intro_sections.heading: Objectives
intro_sections.text: Objectives Understanding use of UNIX pipelines Understanding use of UNIX filters (sed, sort, uniq, cut, tr)

intro_sections.heading: Preparation
intro_sections.text: Preparation Before the lab you should re-read the relevant lecture slides and their accompanying examples.

intro_sections.heading: Getting Started
intro_sections.text: Getting Started Set up for the lab by creating a new directory called lab02 and changing to this directory. mkdir lab02 cd lab02 There are some provided files for this lab which you can fetch with this command: 2041 fetch lab02 If you're not working at CSE, you can download the provided files as a zip file or a tar file .

exercises.label: Exercise
exercises.title: Sorting UNSW Enrolments
intro_paragraphs: There is a template file named sorting_enrolments_answers.txt which you must use to enter the answers for this exercise.
The autotest scripts depend on the format of sorting_enrolments_answers.txt so just add your answers don't otherwise change the file.
The file enrolments.psv contains a list of fake CSE enrolments.
The file enrolments.psv has 9 columns of data (columns are pipe separated):
Each row of data represents one enrolment.

questions.prompt: Write the sort and the head or tail commands needed to print the enrolment for the student with the lowest zID .
extra_paragraphs: If the student with the lowest zID has multiple enrolments, print their enrolment in the course with the highest Course Code .
hint_paragraphs: Note that this is one line.
The same is true for the other parts of this activity.

questions.prompt: Write the sort and the head or tail commands needed to print the first 100 enrolments ordered first by Course Code , then by zID .
Write the sort and the head or tail commands needed to print the first 50 enrolments ordered first by Birthdate , then by Course Code , then by Zid .

questions.prompt: Write the sort and the head or tail commands needed to print the first 25 enrolments ordered first by the decimal part of the WAM in descending order, then by zID in ascending order, then by Course Code also in ascending order.

footer_paragraphs: When you are finished working on this exercise, you must submit your work by running give :
before Monday 02 March 2026 12:00 (midday) (2026-03-02 12:00:00) to obtain the marks for this lab exercise.

exercises.label: Exercise
exercises.title: Counting UNSW classes
intro_paragraphs: There is a template file named counting_classes_answers.txt which you must use to enter the answers for this exercise.
The autotest scripts depend on the format of counting_classes_answers.txt so just add your answers don't otherwise change the file.
The file classes.tsv contains a list of CSE classes.
The file classes.tsv has 7 columns of data (columns are tab separated):
Each row of data represents one class.

questions.prompt: Write a shell pipeline which will print how many classes there are.
Write a shell pipeline which will print how many different courses have classes.
Write a shell pipeline which will print the course with the most classes, and how many classes are in this course.
extra_paragraphs: If there are multiple courses with the same number of classes, print the course that is alphabeticaly first.

questions.prompt: Write a shell pipeline which will print the two rooms most frequently used by non-LAB CSE classes and how often they are used.
extra_paragraphs: If there are multiple rooms that are used by the same number of non-LAB CSE classes, print order them alphabeticaly.

questions.prompt: Write a shell pipeline which will print the most common day in the week and hour in the day for classes to start and how many classes start at that time.
extra_paragraphs: If there are multiple days and times that are used by the same number of classes, print the day and time that is alphabeticaly first.

questions.prompt: Write a shell pipeline which will print the latest time a class will finish.

Write a shell pipeline which will print a list of the course codes of COMP courses that run 2 or more classes of the same type starting at the same time on the same day. (e.g. three tuts starting Monday at 10:00).

exercises.label: Exercise
exercises.title: Editing C Source Files
intro_paragraphs: There is a template file named editing_programs_answers.txt which you must use to enter the answers for this exercise.
The autotest scripts depend on the format of editing_programs_answers.txt so just add your answers don't otherwise change the file.
The file program.c contains a C library implementing some simple sorting algorithms.

questions.prompt: Write a sed command to change all the sort related functions from V1 to V2 . This includes all relevant comments.
Write a sed command to remove all single line comments starting with TODO or FIXME .
Write a sed command to print all lines starting with extern .
hint_code_blocks:
extern int strcmp(const char *s1, const char *s2);
extern int printf(const char *format, ...);

questions.prompt: Write a sed command to replace all include statements using " " with < > .
Write a sed command to remove the main method.

footer_paragraphs: When you are finished working on this exercise, you must submit your work by running give :
before Monday 02 March 2026 12:00 (midday) (2026-03-02 12:00:00) to obtain the marks for this lab exercise.

exercises.label: Challenge Exercise
exercises.title: Exploring Regular Expression Extensions
intro_paragraphs: There is a template file named advanced_ab_answers.txt which you must use to enter the answers for this exercise.
The autotest scripts depend on the format of advanced_ab_answers.txt so just add your answers don't otherwise change the file.
Use grep -P to test your answers to these questions.
These questions can't be solved using the standard regular expression language described in lectures. The following commands may provide useful information:
We've provided a set of test cases in input.txt
intro_code_blocks:
man 1 grep
    info grep
    man 7 regex
    perldoc perlre

questions.prompt: Write a grep -P command that prints the lines in a file named input.txt containing only the characters A and B such that there are exactly n A's followed by exactly n B's and no other characters.
extra_paragraphs: Matching AAABBB AB AABB AAAAAAAAAABBBBBBBBBB Not Matching AAABB ABBBBB AAAAAA AABBAB

footer_paragraphs: When you are finished working on this exercise, you must submit your work by running give :
before Monday 02 March 2026 12:00 (midday) (2026-03-02 12:00:00) to obtain the marks for this lab exercise.
